- SOL dropped another 15-20%
- Total crypto market cap fell by roughly $100 billion in 24 hours

label: 'FTX Has Been Hacked': Crypto Disaster Worsens as Exchange Sees Mysterious Outflows Exceeding $600M
content:
# 'FTX Has Been Hacked': Crypto Disaster Worsens as Exchange Sees Mysterious Outflows Exceeding $600M

By Cheyenne Ligon, Sam Reynolds, Sam Kessler, Nikhilesh De | Edited by Nick Baker
Published Nov 12, 2022, 4:20 a.m. | Updated Jan 10, 2025, 7:51 p.m.

Over $600 million vanished from FTX's crypto wallets hours after the exchange filed for bankruptcy on November 11, 2022. Company officials confirmed the incident through their official Telegram channel, warning users about potential security threats.

An account administrator in the FTX Support Telegram chat posted: "FTX has been hacked. FTX apps are malware. Delete them." The message was pinned by FTX General Counsel Ryne Miller, instructing users to avoid the FTX website as it "might download Trojans."

Hours after the incident, Miller disclosed on Twitter that "FTX US and FTX.com had been moving all their digital assets to cold storage" because of Friday's bankruptcy filing. He noted the process was "expedited this evening – to mitigate damage upon observing unauthorized transactions."

Many FTX wallet holders reported zero balances in their accounts. Various Ethereum tokens, Solana, and Binance Smart Chain tokens exited FTX's official wallets and moved to decentralized exchanges like 1inch. Both FTX and FTX US appeared affected.

Speculation circulated online about whether the outflows represented an external attack or an inside job. Twitter analyst ZachXBT reported that "multiple former FTX employees confirmed to me that they do not recognize these transfers," suggesting potential insider involvement.

Around midnight Eastern time, FTX's login portal became unavailable, displaying a 503 error when users attempted access — typically indicating server unavailability or maintenance.

The Securities Commission of the Bahamas later confirmed on November 18 that it had directed the transfer of FTX digital assets to government-controlled wallets for "safekeeping." The commission said it "exercised its powers as a regulator to protect the interests of clients and creditors of FTX." However, this only accounted for a portion of the total movements.

Blockchain analytics firms including Elliptic and Chainalysis tracked the movements across multiple chains. The draining address was labeled by Etherscan as "FTX Accounts Drainer." Some tokens were swapped through decentralized exchanges — a pattern consistent with attempted laundering — while others were simply moved to new wallets.

The full accounting of which movements were authorized regulatory seizures versus unauthorized theft took months to sort out during the bankruptcy proceedings.

label: FTX Files Chapter 11 Bankruptcy
content:
# FTX Files Chapter 11 Bankruptcy

## SBF Statement

> "I'm sorry. That's the biggest thing. I f***ed up, and should have done better."

SBF posted a lengthy Twitter thread attempting to explain. Key passage:

> "FTX International currently has a total market value of assets/collateral higher than client deposits. But that's different from liquidity — as you can tell, it was not possible to convert that to USD (or other) fast enough."

He maintained that FTX US was solvent. This was later disputed. The criminal trial established that approximately $8 billion in customer funds had been transferred to Alameda and lost.

label: Customer Reactions and Crypto Market Crash — Compiled Reports
content:
# Customer Reactions and Crypto Market Crash — Compiled Reports

## Customer Posts (Reddit, Twitter, Discord)

Approximately 1 million+ FTX customers had funds frozen on the platform.

"I had my entire life savings on FTX. $200,000. I can't access any of it."

"I trusted SBF because he testified before Congress and talked about effective altruism. I feel like the biggest idiot in the world."

"Three days ago SBF said assets are fine. I believed him and didn't withdraw. Now I've lost everything."

"I had my business payroll on FTX US. SBF said US entity was solvent. My employees need to get paid next week."

Losses reported ranged from hundreds to millions of dollars. Some users explicitly chose NOT to withdraw because they trusted SBF's "assets are fine" tweet.

label: Customer Reactions and Crypto Market Crash — Compiled Reports
content:
# Customer Reactions and Crypto Market Crash — Compiled Reports

## Crypto Market Data

BTC price:
- Nov 6: ~$21,000
- Nov 9: ~$15,700 (two-year low)
- Nov 14: ~$16,300 (partial stabilization)
- Total decline: ~25% in a week

ETH:
- Similar ~25% decline
- Brief dip below $1,100

Total crypto market cap lost approximately $200 billion in the week following CZ's tweet.

label: Customer Reactions and Crypto Market Crash — Compiled Reports
content:
# Customer Reactions and Crypto Market Crash — Compiled Reports

## Contagion

- **BlockFi**: $680M exposure to FTX/Alameda. Filed for bankruptcy Nov 28.
- **Genesis Lending**: Halted withdrawals Nov 16 due to FTX exposure. Parent company DCG entered restructuring.
- **Gemini Earn**: Froze withdrawals due to Genesis exposure.
- **Various smaller funds and lenders**: Dozens of crypto firms disclosed FTX exposure.

Total destruction of value (direct + contagion) ran into hundreds of billions.

label: United States v. Samuel Bankman-Fried — DOJ / Trial Report
content:
# United States v. Samuel Bankman-Fried — DOJ / Trial Report

## Arrest and Indictment

December 12, 2022 — Sam Bankman-Fried arrested in the Bahamas at the request of the U.S. government. Indicted by federal grand jury, Southern District of New York, December 13.

Charges:
- Wire fraud (2 counts)
- Conspiracy to commit wire fraud (2 counts)
- Conspiracy to commit commodities fraud
- Conspiracy to commit securities fraud
- Conspiracy to commit money laundering
- Campaign finance violations

label: United States v. Samuel Bankman-Fried — DOJ / Trial Report
content:
# United States v. Samuel Bankman-Fried — DOJ / Trial Report

## Trial — October 2023

Trial ran October 3 to November 2, 2023, Manhattan federal court.

**Caroline Ellison** (Alameda CEO, cooperating witness):
- SBF directed her to use FTX customer funds to cover Alameda's losses
- Alameda had a secret backdoor exemption from FTX's liquidation engine
- SBF told her to create misleading balance sheets for lenders
- ~$8 billion in customer funds transferred to Alameda

**Gary Wang** (FTX co-founder, cooperating witness):
- Wrote the code that gave Alameda the special exemption
- Described the "allow_negative" flag that let Alameda borrow unlimited customer funds
- SBF directed the technical implementation

**Nishad Singh** (FTX engineering director, cooperating witness):
- Corroborated the other witnesses
- Described concerns he raised that were dismissed by SBF

label: United States v. Samuel Bankman-Fried — DOJ / Trial Report
content:
# United States v. Samuel Bankman-Fried — DOJ / Trial Report

## Verdict

November 2, 2023. Guilty on all 7 counts:

1. Wire fraud on FTX customers
2. Conspiracy to commit wire fraud on FTX customers
3. Wire fraud on Alameda Research lenders
4. Conspiracy to commit wire fraud on Alameda lenders
5. Conspiracy to commit securities fraud on FTX investors
6. Conspiracy to commit commodities fraud
7. Conspiracy to commit money laundering

label: United States v. Samuel Bankman-Fried — DOJ / Trial Report
content:
# United States v. Samuel Bankman-Fried — DOJ / Trial Report

## Sentencing

March 28, 2024 — 25 years in federal prison.

Judge noted SBF showed no remorse and had perjured himself during trial.

label: United States v. Samuel Bankman-Fried — DOJ / Trial Report

label: Regulatory Responses to FTX Collapse — Compiled Press Releases
content:
# Regulatory Responses to FTX Collapse — Compiled Press Releases

## SEC — December 13, 2022

Securities and Exchange Commission charged SBF:

> "From at least May 2019 through November 2022, Bankman-Fried raised more than $1.8 billion from equity investors, including approximately $1 billion from approximately 90 U.S.-based investors. He did so by promoting FTX as a safe, responsible crypto asset trading platform. In reality, Bankman-Fried orchestrated a massive, years-long fraud, diverting billions of dollars of FTX customer funds for his own personal benefit and to help grow his crypto empire."

SEC Chair Gary Gensler:

> "We allege that Sam Bankman-Fried built a house of cards on a foundation of deception while telling investors that it was one of the safest buildings in crypto."

label: Regulatory Responses to FTX Collapse — Compiled Press Releases
content:
# Regulatory Responses to FTX Collapse — Compiled Press Releases

## CFTC

Commodity Futures Trading Commission complaint and judgment:
- $12.7 billion total judgment
- $8.7 billion in restitution for victims
- $4.0 billion in disgorgement

CFTC Commissioner Kristin Johnson:

> "FTX touted itself as 'the safest and easiest way to buy and sell crypto,' and stated FTX segregated customer assets from FTX's own assets as a general principle, when in fact customer funds were commingled and misappropriated."

label: Regulatory Responses to FTX Collapse — Compiled Press Releases
content:
# Regulatory Responses to FTX Collapse — Compiled Press Releases

## Global Regulatory Acceleration

- **EU**: MiCA (Markets in Crypto-Assets) regulation finalized and enacted, taking effect 2024
- **US**: Multiple congressional hearings, SEC enforcement actions against other exchanges (Binance, Coinbase), proposed legislation
- **UK**: FCA stepped up crypto oversight
- **Hong Kong**: New licensing framework for crypto exchanges
- **Singapore**: MAS tightened requirements
- **Japan**: Already had strict requirements; FTX Japan customers were relatively protected due to local segregation rules

label: Regulatory Responses to FTX Collapse — Compiled Press Releases
content:
# Regulatory Responses to FTX Collapse — Compiled Press Releases

## Cooperating Witness Sentences

1. Caroline Ellison (Alameda CEO) — 2 years
2. Gary Wang (FTX co-founder) — no prison time
3. Nishad Singh (engineering director) — no prison time
4. Ryan Salame (FTX co-CEO) — 7.5 years

label: Regulatory Responses to FTX Collapse — Compiled Press Releases
content:
# Regulatory Responses to FTX Collapse — Compiled Press Releases

## FTX Estate Recovery

Under John Ray III's management:
- Recovered approximately $14.5-16 billion in assets
- Creditors expected to receive roughly 119% of claims (at USD value as of November 2022 filing date)
- At current crypto prices, recovery represents a much lower percentage of what the crypto would have been worth
- Recovery came from selling FTX venture investments, recovering Bahamas assets, liquidating remaining crypto

label: SBF Trial: What Did FTX's Terms of Service Say About Customer Funds?
content:
# SBF Trial: What Did FTX's Terms of Service Say About Customer Funds?

By Nikhilesh De | Edited by Marc Hochstein
Published: October 16, 2023

Sam Bankman-Fried's lawyers say FTX didn't misuse customer funds under its terms of service. We dug into the agreement to see exactly what it says.

---

Phase one of the trial was exciting. Former FTX exchange insiders told a jury that Sam Bankman-Fried had directed them to let his hedge fund Alameda "borrow" billions of dollars from exchange customers in a secret arrangement facilitated through internal backdoors and hidden behind falsified documents.

We seem to be moving into phase two now, with witnesses who played secondary roles in this massive alleged fraud. Friday's testimony from BlockFi CEO Zac Prince was important but dare I say almost boring. He and other upcoming witnesses are not likely privy to salacious details like "Sam told me to commit crimes." There are two exceptions, which I'll get to below, but our next few witnesses are customers, an FBI agent and an Alameda employee who may or may have also been an FTX US human resources employee(?).

So let's talk about the defense again instead. We're still waiting for the defense's strategy to become clear. We've gotten hints, as Axios' Crystal Kim points out.

A filing last week suggests the defense wants to get back to the argument that Bankman-Fried did not technically commit wire fraud because FTX's terms of service were worded in such a way that there's no case to argue funds were misappropriated.

We've heard this argument a few times already from the defense team, so I dug into what the terms of service actually said.

First off: Judge Lewis Kaplan, who's overseeing the case, has already rejected one proposed defense witness, English barrister (basically British for "lawyer") Lawrence Akka, from offering testimony because the defense wanted this individual to explain the English laws governing FTX's terms to the jury, and explaining the law to the jury is the judge's job.

In its new filing, the defense team continues to argue that FTX's relationship with its customers "was governed by [its] terms of service," and so "the question of whether Alameda's use of customer fiat deposits was permissible within the scope of that relationship -- and, thus, whether a theft of property occurred -- is governed by foreign law," at least with regard to counts one and three of the indictment (referring to the actual wire fraud, i.e. substantive, charges).

"It is the defense's position that the rights and obligations of parties to a commercial relationship are not established by their expectations and understandings for purposes of the misappropriation theory of the federal fraud statutes," the defense's Oct. 12 went on to say.

So I dug up a PDF of FTX's terms of service from May 2022.

According to that document, section 8.2.6, which governs FTX customers' digital assets, holds that the "title to your digital assets shall at all times remain with you and shall not transfer to FTX trading." While customers can't blame FTX for loss in value due to price fluctuations, that's not what the DOJ is alleging here.

"None of the digital assets in your account are the property of, or shall or may be loans to, FTX Trading; FTX Trading does not represent or treat Digital Assets in the User's Accounts as belonging to FTX Trading," section 8.2.6 (B) said.

I didn't see the part of the DOJ filing which highlighted this section, but this section seems to contradict what the defense team is claiming in its Oct. 12 letter.

Then I took a look at the defense's Sept. 11 letter arguing it should be able to call up Akka to explain the meaning of the terms under English law.

That letter made it clear that in the defense's view, the DOJ is "relying on certain provisions in FTX's terms of service," suggesting there are other provisions that do undermine the misappropriation claim.

The Sept. 11 letter referenced a Sept. 1 filing asking for permission to introduce the entire terms as an exhibit (as opposed to just section 8.2), which in turn seemed to suggest that the question of misappropriation goes strictly to how FTX used customer fiat currency (emphasis mine).

"Among other things, the Terms of Service are relevant to whether FTX's or Alameda's use of customer fiat currency was inconsistent with representations to customers in the document itself and rights and obligations it sets forth regarding customer assets -- and thus to whether there was any actual fraud or 'misappropriation' of customer funds," the Sept. 1 filing said.

And sure enough, the "fiat currency" section of the terms of service, 8.3, is much more vague about how FTX held customer funds. FTX said it may convert fiat deposits into "e-money," which the terms describe as a sort of internal accounting tool.

There are no clauses in this section addressing how FTX might hold customer deposits, unlike the digital assets section.

But is the DOJ only alleging that FTX stole customers' fiat funds? No. It's not. Assistant U.S. Attorney Thane Rehn, in his opening statement, told the jury that Bankman-Fried let Alameda withdraw FTX customers' crypto (emphasis still mine).

"Using Alameda, the defendant withdrew billions of dollars worth of customer crypto out of the FTX digital wallet and into Alameda's digital wallet that he owned and controlled," he alleged.

Unsurprisingly, the DOJ disagrees with the defense's Oct. 12 argument. In its own filing, which seems to have dropped late Friday (Oct. 13), prosecutors say that "misappropriation occurs when a party breaches a 'fiduciary duty.'" Prosecutors have already spent the early days of the trial pointing out that Bankman-Fried, through advertisements (including the infamous Super Bowl ad with Larry David) and other public appearances, said FTX was a safe and "trusted way to buy and sell" cryptocurrencies.

The DOJ filing also said "the defendant is simply wrong" that prosecutors are just arguing misappropriation. They're also alleging Bankman-Fried made "material misrepresentations and omissions."

The DOJ's Oct. 13 filing, interestingly, did not bring up the whole English law question, though prosecutors did express their opposition to that argument in a late August filing. At the time, the DOJ said that "framing the issue as a dichotomy" between the terms of service and other legal structures may be misleading for the jury.

Speaking of FTX's terms of service, it's maybe worth highlighting that on page one, the document said, "Affiliates of FTX Trading may execute trades on the Platform provided, however, that such Affiliates shall not be afforded any priority in trade execution."

Former FTX Chief Technology Officer Gary Wang told the court that Alameda Research "had the ability to place orders slightly faster than other accounts."

There is also a section on page 22 where the terms say "nothing in the terms shall limit or exclude a party's liability ... for fraud or fraudulent misrepresentation."
